The second and third falls were lost when the railway was laid, but the first falls remain in Takekura, Mishima (old name: Takekura){{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. Another tradition says worship began from water springing from the "sword-like stone bed" at that Takekura site{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The plateau behind the shrine, now divided by a road, was formerly continuous with the shrine grounds, and ancient tomb groups have been confirmed on that plateau{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. From this, it is inferred that ritual worship was conducted here since the Kofun period{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, the shikinaisha "Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Tagata District|Q1154841|WD=Q1154841|1=Tagata District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, is identified with this shrine{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}. However, documentary evidence showing this identification descends only to a munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1502|Q6137|WD=Q6137|1=1502}}){{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. If this shrine is that shikinaisha, the stone beds and cliffs of the first, second, and third falls are said to represent the "sword/blade stone bed"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The reading is annotated "Tsurugitachi Iwatoko" in the Kujō family manuscript, while other manuscripts give "Kemutono Iwatoko"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. In medieval {{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}, the entry "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}} is identified with this shrine. By the late medieval period it had syncretized with mountain ascetic worship, {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}, and was also called "Ontake Daigongen"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. Other detailed history is unclear, but the shrine preserves munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}, {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, {{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754|1=Genbun}}, {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}, {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}}, etc., and displays a torii plaque from {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in the modern shrine ranking system{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. During the Meiji separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this shrine was called "Ontake Shrine," but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 23 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}) it was renamed to the present "Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. It is also colloquially called "Yata Gomonsha"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Divine rank === * Junior Fourth Rank Upper (''{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}'') - "Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. === Subsidiary shrines === * Yasaka Shrine * Akiba Shrine * Yamanokami Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yasaka & Akiba-jinja.JPG|Yasaka Shrine / Akiba Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yamanokami-jinja.JPG|Yamanokami Shrine === Festivals === Rites are held on {{ill|lt=January 15|Q2260|WD=Q2260|1=January 15}} and {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}; the latter is the annual festivalExplanatory board in the precincts.. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === References === {{Commonscat|Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} * Local explanatory board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Association|author=Takashio Hiroshi|chapter=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|year=1981|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 10|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Mishima City History Compilation Committee|author=|year=1988|title=Mishima City History Vol. 2|publisher=Mishima City|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Yata Village|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22: Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:三島市の歴史]] [[Category:三島市の建築物]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]]

[[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja shaden.JPG|250px]]
Shrine buildings |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}Yata 293 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto
{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=6|lat_sec=46.9|lon_deg=138|lon_min=56|lon_sec=1.3|label=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]] '''Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみことじんじゃName and reading from [http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)., also written '''Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine''') is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yata, {{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The two enshrined deities are as follows{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. * Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみこと) * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (やまとたけるのみこと) *: The origin of enshrining Yamato Takeru is unknown; it is presumed to derive from eastern expedition legends of Yamato Takeru{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === History === === Foundation === Shrine tradition says that worship of waterfalls called "First Falls," "Second Falls," and "Third Falls" was the origin of this shrine{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. The second and third falls were lost when the railway was laid, but the first falls remain in Takekura, Mishima (old name: Takekura){{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. Another tradition says worship began from water springing from the "sword-like stone bed" at that Takekura site{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The plateau behind the shrine, now divided by a road, was formerly continuous with the shrine grounds, and ancient tomb groups have been confirmed on that plateau{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. From this, it is inferred that ritual worship was conducted here since the Kofun period{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, the shikinaisha "Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Tagata District|Q1154841|WD=Q1154841|1=Tagata District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, is identified with this shrine{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}. However, documentary evidence showing this identification descends only to a munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1502|Q6137|WD=Q6137|1=1502}}){{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. If this shrine is that shikinaisha, the stone beds and cliffs of the first, second, and third falls are said to represent the "sword/blade stone bed"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The reading is annotated "Tsurugitachi Iwatoko" in the Kujō family manuscript, while other manuscripts give "Kemutono Iwatoko"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. In medieval {{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}, the entry "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}} is identified with this shrine. By the late medieval period it had syncretized with mountain ascetic worship, {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}, and was also called "Ontake Daigongen"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. Other detailed history is unclear, but the shrine preserves munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}, {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, {{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754|1=Genbun}}, {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}, {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}}, etc., and displays a torii plaque from {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in the modern shrine ranking system{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. During the Meiji separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this shrine was called "Ontake Shrine," but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 23 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}) it was renamed to the present "Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. It is also colloquially called "Yata Gomonsha"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Divine rank === * Junior Fourth Rank Upper (''{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}'') - "Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. === Subsidiary shrines === * Yasaka Shrine * Akiba Shrine * Yamanokami Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yasaka & Akiba-jinja.JPG|Yasaka Shrine / Akiba Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yamanokami-jinja.JPG|Yamanokami Shrine === Festivals === Rites are held on {{ill|lt=January 15|Q2260|WD=Q2260|1=January 15}} and {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}; the latter is the annual festivalExplanatory board in the precincts.. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === References === {{Commonscat|Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} * Local explanatory board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Association|author=Takashio Hiroshi|chapter=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|year=1981|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 10|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Mishima City History Compilation Committee|author=|year=1988|title=Mishima City History Vol. 2|publisher=Mishima City|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Yata Village|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22: Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:三島市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Mishima, Shizuoka]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]]

{{commons category|Category:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} [[JA:劒刀石床別命神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17229202}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine |画像 = [[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja shaden.JPG|250px]]
Shrine buildings |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}Yata 293 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto
{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=6|lat_sec=46.9|lon_deg=138|lon_min=56|lon_sec=1.3|label=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]] '''Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみことじんじゃName and reading from [http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)., also written '''Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine''') is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yata, {{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The two enshrined deities are as follows{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. * Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみこと) * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (やまとたけるのみこと) *: The origin of enshrining Yamato Takeru is unknown; it is presumed to derive from eastern expedition legends of Yamato Takeru{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === History === === Foundation === Shrine tradition says that worship of waterfalls called "First Falls," "Second Falls," and "Third Falls" was the origin of this shrine{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. The second and third falls were lost when the railway was laid, but the first falls remain in Takekura, Mishima (old name: Takekura){{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. Another tradition says worship began from water springing from the "sword-like stone bed" at that Takekura site{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The plateau behind the shrine, now divided by a road, was formerly continuous with the shrine grounds, and ancient tomb groups have been confirmed on that plateau{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. From this, it is inferred that ritual worship was conducted here since the Kofun period{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, the shikinaisha "Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Tagata District|Q1154841|WD=Q1154841|1=Tagata District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, is identified with this shrine{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}. However, documentary evidence showing this identification descends only to a munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1502|Q6137|WD=Q6137|1=1502}}){{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. If this shrine is that shikinaisha, the stone beds and cliffs of the first, second, and third falls are said to represent the "sword/blade stone bed"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The reading is annotated "Tsurugitachi Iwatoko" in the Kujō family manuscript, while other manuscripts give "Kemutono Iwatoko"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. In medieval {{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}, the entry "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}} is identified with this shrine. By the late medieval period it had syncretized with mountain ascetic worship, {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}, and was also called "Ontake Daigongen"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. Other detailed history is unclear, but the shrine preserves munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}, {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, {{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754|1=Genbun}}, {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}, {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}}, etc., and displays a torii plaque from {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in the modern shrine ranking system{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. During the Meiji separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this shrine was called "Ontake Shrine," but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 23 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}) it was renamed to the present "Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. It is also colloquially called "Yata Gomonsha"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Divine rank === * Junior Fourth Rank Upper (''{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}'') - "Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. === Subsidiary shrines === * Yasaka Shrine * Akiba Shrine * Yamanokami Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yasaka & Akiba-jinja.JPG|Yasaka Shrine / Akiba Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yamanokami-jinja.JPG|Yamanokami Shrine === Festivals === Rites are held on {{ill|lt=January 15|Q2260|WD=Q2260|1=January 15}} and {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}; the latter is the annual festivalExplanatory board in the precincts.. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === References === {{Commonscat|Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} * Local explanatory board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Association|author=Takashio Hiroshi|chapter=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|year=1981|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 10|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Mishima City History Compilation Committee|author=|year=1988|title=Mishima City History Vol. 2|publisher=Mishima City|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Yata Village|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22: Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:三島市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Mishima, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]]

former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=6|lat_sec=46.9|lon_deg=138|lon_min=56|lon_sec=1.3|label=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]] '''Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみことじんじゃName and reading from [http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)., also written '''Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine''') is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yata, {{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The two enshrined deities are as follows{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. * Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみこと) * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (やまとたけるのみこと) *: The origin of enshrining Yamato Takeru is unknown; it is presumed to derive from eastern expedition legends of Yamato Takeru{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === History === === Foundation === Shrine tradition says that worship of waterfalls called "First Falls," "Second Falls," and "Third Falls" was the origin of this shrine{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. The second and third falls were lost when the railway was laid, but the first falls remain in Takekura, Mishima (old name: Takekura){{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. Another tradition says worship began from water springing from the "sword-like stone bed" at that Takekura site{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The plateau behind the shrine, now divided by a road, was formerly continuous with the shrine grounds, and ancient tomb groups have been confirmed on that plateau{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. From this, it is inferred that ritual worship was conducted here since the Kofun period{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, the shikinaisha "Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Tagata District|Q1154841|WD=Q1154841|1=Tagata District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, is identified with this shrine{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}. However, documentary evidence showing this identification descends only to a munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1502|Q6137|WD=Q6137|1=1502}}){{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. If this shrine is that shikinaisha, the stone beds and cliffs of the first, second, and third falls are said to represent the "sword/blade stone bed"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The reading is annotated "Tsurugitachi Iwatoko" in the Kujō family manuscript, while other manuscripts give "Kemutono Iwatoko"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. In medieval {{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}, the entry "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}} is identified with this shrine. By the late medieval period it had syncretized with mountain ascetic worship, {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}, and was also called "Ontake Daigongen"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. Other detailed history is unclear, but the shrine preserves munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}, {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, {{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754|1=Genbun}}, {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}, {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}}, etc., and displays a torii plaque from {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in the modern shrine ranking system{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. During the Meiji separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this shrine was called "Ontake Shrine," but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 23 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}) it was renamed to the present "Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. It is also colloquially called "Yata Gomonsha"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Divine rank === * Junior Fourth Rank Upper (''{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}'') - "Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. === Subsidiary shrines === * Yasaka Shrine * Akiba Shrine * Yamanokami Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yasaka & Akiba-jinja.JPG|Yasaka Shrine / Akiba Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yamanokami-jinja.JPG|Yamanokami Shrine === Festivals === Rites are held on {{ill|lt=January 15|Q2260|WD=Q2260|1=January 15}} and {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}; the latter is the annual festivalExplanatory board in the precincts.. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === References === {{Commonscat|Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} * Local explanatory board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Association|author=Takashio Hiroshi|chapter=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|year=1981|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 10|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Mishima City History Compilation Committee|author=|year=1988|title=Mishima City History Vol. 2|publisher=Mishima City|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Yata Village|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22: Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:三島市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Mishima, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]]

In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in the modern shrine ranking system{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. During the Meiji separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this shrine was called "Ontake Shrine," but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 23 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}) it was renamed to the present "Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. It is also colloquially called "Yata Gomonsha"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Divine rank === * Junior Fourth Rank Upper (''{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}'') - "Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. === Subsidiary shrines === * Yasaka Shrine * Akiba Shrine * Yamanokami Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yasaka & Akiba-jinja.JPG|Yasaka Shrine / Akiba Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yamanokami-jinja.JPG|Yamanokami Shrine === Festivals === Rites are held on {{ill|lt=January 15|Q2260|WD=Q2260|1=January 15}} and {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}; the latter is the annual festivalExplanatory board in the precincts.. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === References === {{Commonscat|Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} * Local explanatory board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Association|author=Takashio Hiroshi|chapter=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|year=1981|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 10|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Mishima City History Compilation Committee|author=|year=1988|title=Mishima City History Vol. 2|publisher=Mishima City|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Yata Village|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22: Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:三島市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Mishima, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]]

{{commons category|Category:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} [[JA:劒刀石床別命神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17229202}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine |画像 = [[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja shaden.JPG|250px]]
Shrine buildings |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}Yata 293 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto
{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|lat_deg=35|lat_min=6|lat_sec=46.9|lon_deg=138|lon_min=56|lon_sec=1.3|label=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|position=left|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|6|46.9|N|138|56|1.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]] '''Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみことじんじゃName and reading from [http://shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)., also written '''Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine''') is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yata, {{ill|lt=Mishima|Q653478|WD=Q653478|1=Mishima, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the former {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} its rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The two enshrined deities are as follows{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. * Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto (つるぎたちいわとこわけのみこと) * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (やまとたけるのみこと) *: The origin of enshrining Yamato Takeru is unknown; it is presumed to derive from eastern expedition legends of Yamato Takeru{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === History === === Foundation === Shrine tradition says that worship of waterfalls called "First Falls," "Second Falls," and "Third Falls" was the origin of this shrine{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. The second and third falls were lost when the railway was laid, but the first falls remain in Takekura, Mishima (old name: Takekura){{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. Another tradition says worship began from water springing from the "sword-like stone bed" at that Takekura site{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The plateau behind the shrine, now divided by a road, was formerly continuous with the shrine grounds, and ancient tomb groups have been confirmed on that plateau{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. From this, it is inferred that ritual worship was conducted here since the Kofun period{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Overview === In the {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Engi|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, the shikinaisha "Ken-tō Iwatoko-wake-no-Mikoto Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Tagata District|Q1154841|WD=Q1154841|1=Tagata District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, is identified with this shrine{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}. However, documentary evidence showing this identification descends only to a munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1502|Q6137|WD=Q6137|1=1502}}){{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. If this shrine is that shikinaisha, the stone beds and cliffs of the first, second, and third falls are said to represent the "sword/blade stone bed"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. The reading is annotated "Tsurugitachi Iwatoko" in the Kujō family manuscript, while other manuscripts give "Kemutono Iwatoko"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. In medieval {{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}, the entry "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}} is identified with this shrine. By the late medieval period it had syncretized with mountain ascetic worship, {{ill|lt=Shugendō|Q1483875|WD=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}, and was also called "Ontake Daigongen"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. Other detailed history is unclear, but the shrine preserves munafuda from {{ill|lt=Bunki|Q1324885|WD=Q1324885|1=Bunki}}, {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, {{ill|lt=Genbun|Q1145754|WD=Q1145754|1=Genbun}}, {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}, {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|1=Kaei}}, etc., and displays a torii plaque from {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} in the modern shrine ranking system{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. During the Meiji separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this shrine was called "Ontake Shrine," but in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 23 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}) it was renamed to the present "Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. It is also colloquially called "Yata Gomonsha"{{Sfn|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}. === Divine rank === * Junior Fourth Rank Upper (''{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkai-chō}}'') - "Takiyama no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}} or "Natsume no Myōjin"{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}. === Subsidiary shrines === * Yasaka Shrine * Akiba Shrine * Yamanokami Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yasaka & Akiba-jinja.JPG|Yasaka Shrine / Akiba Shrine File:Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja Yamanokami-jinja.JPG|Yamanokami Shrine === Festivals === Rites are held on {{ill|lt=January 15|Q2260|WD=Q2260|1=January 15}} and {{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}{{Sfn|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988|p=561}}; the latter is the annual festivalExplanatory board in the precincts.. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === References === {{Commonscat|Tsurugitachi-iwatokowakenomikoto-jinja}} * Local explanatory board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Association|author=Takashio Hiroshi|chapter=Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine|year=1981|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Vol. 10|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shikinaisha Survey Report|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Mishima City History Compilation Committee|author=|year=1988|title=Mishima City History Vol. 2|publisher=Mishima City|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Mishima City History Vol. 2|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2000|chapter=Yata Village|title=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22: Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490220|ref={{Harvid|Place Names of Shizuoka Prefecture|2000}}}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4402023 Tsurugitachi Iwatokowake-no-Mikoto Shrine] - Shizuoka Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tsurugitachi Iwato Kowake No Mikoto Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:三島市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Mishima, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]]
